transcript.text: Hey, good morning, Simone.

I'm doing great.

Yeah.
Fair enough.

Yeah, perfect.

San Francisco and the Marina.
What about you?
Okay, nice.

Ah, okay.
Cool, cool.
Yeah, so it's nice.
I get to go back and forth, and it's like I never left.
And it makes me less depressed about the weather in Houston.
Yeah, is it bad?

Oh, that you come to San Francisco in the summer, it is so cold.
Yeah, like Mark Twain says, right?
Exactly, exactly.
That apparently it was not him.
Oh, yeah?
Who had the quote originally?
Some random guy?
Yeah, I think there's some random guy that nobody knows, but somehow people start saying it was Mark Twain, and now it's Mark Twain.
But I think the history goes that this was not him.
Like, the only reason I know is because I asked CHGPT, and without CHGPT I would not have done this research.
That's what CHGPT says.
There we go.
AI evolving us, making us smarter every single day.
Well, it's great to meet you both.

For context, I'm the founding AE here at GrowthX, so I work hand-in-hand with our CEO, Marcel, and really just, you know, hoping to use our time today to learn more about the why now for, right, like...
SEO, GEO content, what you'd be looking for at a potential partner, and then give you as much clarity as I can around the way that we work, content we've created.
I'll even go under the hood and show you how the workflows are involved.
Rahul, Simone, maybe you want to start off with just quick intros and a little more context, and we'll go from there.
Yeah, well, I mean, I just joined the company about five months ago, and right now I'm meeting Rahul with growth, demand generation.
So I'm always interested in learning what's new out there, as far as growth tooling and AI.
It's one of my passions.
And yeah, Rahul just told me that I met you and you have something really interesting to show us, like, okay, jump on the call and see.
So I'm really curious to know what you guys do and what is different from other folks.
Awesome.
Great to meet you.
And Tyler, Rahul here.
I've with the company for more than three years now, and the initial SEO growth came from a lot of good content.
Previously, then when I came on board, we had an agency who does the technical content, so we grew that.
But then with AI chats coming in, we are kind of flattened out in terms of organic growth.
So that was one of the reasons I wanted to talk with you guys.
And the way I discovered you was, so Rajan is one of your investors, I think.
Rajan Seth.
And I landed on his profile, and some post stopped me from him, I think.
And then I was like, oh, what's this growthx?
And then I dug into it, and I was like, okay, let's at least chat with
And let's see how you can help.
So yeah, based here in the Bay Area.
Okay, awesome.
Awesome.
And I'm guessing, Simone, the office isn't in the marina or is it?
No, it's actually in the Salesforce Tower.
Oh, cool.
Cool.
Rahul, whereabouts are you in the city?
No, I'm in San Mateo, so close to the airport.
Okay, nice.
Nice.
I was telling Simone that I lived in SF for three years and the GrowthX office is in South Park near the Giants Stadium.
So I get to go a little bit back and forth from Houston.
Yeah, cool.
Oh, so you're in Houston?
Yes, yes.
Cool, cool.
Do you know some people in Houston?
Or have been there?
No, I've been back in 2013, I think.
Just visiting, but I don't know any people there.
there.
Yeah.
Yeah, it's an underrated place.
Everybody knows it because it's one of the most populated cities in America, but it's much more diverse than people realize.
You know, they connect it to just like oil and gas.
And the reality is we've got a bunch of great food of all different cuisines and really rich, you know, art and music scene in Houston.
So always talk highly of it, especially when people are comparing it to Dallas.
A little bit of a rivalry.
Oh, yeah, yeah.
And I still remember this restaurant.
I forgot the name, but it was like an African fusion food.
And it was really good.
It was really good.
There you go.
So you know what it's all about.
Well, just to give like some context on GrowthX, and then we can dive a bit deeper into like your specific opportunity.
We're an end-to-end partner for SEO and GEO content initially.

Well,
Do everything from help you identify the right strategy.
We'll publish daily in most scenarios.
So we target five articles per week, either net new or refresh.
We'll handle the staging and publishing and your CMS.
So all the last mile execution.
And then we'll deliver all of the LLM visibility and monitoring.
And so we use like an internal platform with our team of experts today.
But we're actually going to be launching a product for the AI visibility side.
And we'll use that in our engagements.

And then we'll also, over time, hasn't really been decided upon the timing yet, bring you into the platform where the workflows live so that your team can start to learn how to leverage AI to create content internally.
But today, we're an extension of your team, right?
And the idea is that we free up your content creators, your marketing folks.
We free up their bandwidth so that instead of SEO and GEO content, which requires a lot of scale.
They can focus on your pillar pieces, annual, quarterly, case studies, product marketing, things of that nature.
Any questions just high level about what we do?
I'm happy to elaborate or get specific.
Yeah, if you can tell us a little bit more about the specific and how it works.
And yeah, you mentioned like posting on our CMS, like even five times a day.
Now we probably do five times a month.
So how are the companies, users even be able to consume so much content?
And how do you even come up with ideas and stuff to post five times a day?
Yeah, five times a week, not five times a day.
five times a week.
So sorry.
five times a week.
It's still a lot, like once a day.
Yeah, still a lot for sure.
And I would say that the background, you all understand the opportunity with AI search, but the background.
The is that our founder and CEO, he's really built organic content engines at every stop in his career.
And what he did as the CMO at Deepgram, where they 24x their traffic, 4x their revenue, is he found the places where you could use complex AI workflows to accelerate.
And so if you think about generating different assignments or opportunities, right, you can use AI there, putting together a really robust brief for that content assignment.
Doing deep research, doing it in an agentic way, fact-checking, drafting.
We definitely use AI in several pieces of the process, but our thing that we try to solve for up front in our engagement is getting the context engineering right.
And so we can do technical content.
We definitely have, like, a lot of clients who are B2B, very technical.
But what we're focusing on first is, like, what are those quick wins where we can create, like, the best...
Answer to your audience's top questions, right?
So if you think about like SEO content in general, you're typically like not going too, too, too technical at the beginning.
And so that's typically something that we're doing as we become better experts on your business and your products.
And after we've refined the context that like feeds into all these different workflows.
I'll go deeper, but yeah, I like the back and forth here.
What questions come into mind next, Simone or Rahul?
So one thing on my side, Tyler, is we are very technical solution.
And even though we play in like Kubernetes, Kubernetes is widespread.
We still are like kind of niche player.
It's not like databases and storage.
not storage.
for you're just like, there's there's like, I'm you.
Meaning, every company needs that type of thing.
It's like a bread and butter, basic infra for any company.
It's not like that yet.
So with that said, how do you think the, one thing is the quantity, also the quality of the content.
Do you have like full-time writers on the team?
Do you source it externally, their profiles, especially like infra and communities and GPU and AI, infra is quite, quite complex.
So yeah, mainly that, that question.
Yeah, great, great question.
We have all of our writers in-house, our delivery team, our company is about like 90 employees, 80 employees.
Our delivery team is like two thirds of that.
So those are all the people that work on the content strategy or the execution.
Maybe a good like customer example would be Redis.
We've been working with Redis, and Redis is fairly technical.
Some other good examples here, and then we'll get into content specifically, Abnormal Security, Airbytes, Sentinel-1, Surge AI.

So I think we have found a sweet spot there, but to your point, if it's a niche, there has to be enough search volume and content opportunity for us to be a great partner and really help you drive results.

So I guess just help me understand a little bit better, like that niche that you play in as someone who isn't super technical, like who are you selling to and like what kind of customers are you typically attracting?
Yeah, yeah.
So think of, so VCluster is our flagship product, and we are all in with VCluster.
VCluster stands for virtual clusters.
So the way VMware virtualize physical infrastructure, we are virtualizing Kubernetes.
friends.
That's like a very high-level thing.
Kubernetes has become de facto standard.
Literally all small companies, large companies, Fortune 500, Fortune 100, everybody uses Kubernetes.
But it's not efficient operationally.
It's hard.
So why not kind of virtualize it?
And I won't go into details of like how we virtualize it and things.

But think of it like instead of creating hundreds of thousands of Kubernetes real clusters, anywhere, why not utilize maybe 10 and virtualize those, give developer better experience, give your infra teams better operational experience, save cost, save operational headaches, but think of it like at a high level virtualization for Kubernetes.
And then there are like number of use cases, GPU and AI has become kind of like one of the important ones in recent days.
And we mainly sell into...
So platform teams, like platform engineering teams is like not a new concept, but some teams, some companies have dedicated platform engineering teams.

Some sit under IT infra, some sit under something else, but think of it like platform architects, cloud architects, cloud operations, platform operations, infrastructure architects, a little bit of overlap into like DevOps and SRE type of roles.
But especially the decision makers are into the infra and platform teams.
Super helpful.
And then what types of companies would like benefit from the cluster?
Is it anybody using Kubernetes or is it only companies of a certain size?
So if you think about like total addressable market, yes, it's a horizontal product.
Anybody using Kubernetes, they can use it.
But then we do focus on digital native.
was GPU-first companies, financial services, insurance, a little bit of Department of Defense, B2B, SaaS.
But again, they need to be digital-native in terms of forward-thinking, cloud-native-first type of thing.
So that's our sweet spot.
Okay.
Okay.

Yeah, I think to get a little tactical into, like, the types of content we've created and, you know, what I saw just looking through some of your index pages, you know, I think that looking at some of the content that's driving the most traffic from the blog today, I feel like, you know, either of these pieces represent, like, content that we could create.
You know, definitely the cloud development introductory guide would just confirm with the team around this one.
But just, you know, low-hanging fruit would be, like, refreshing this content.
I recommend with AI search every three months being able to refresh, right, and then update the publishing date.

And then when you think about content that we've created for our customers, the best example to dig into would probably be the data engineering resources we created for Airbyte because we just started publishing for Reddit.
So there's not like a huge sample size there.
And then Augment Code, we've created hundreds of these guides.
It's more enterprise AI coding, but you'll see that in this content, we go pretty deep and we're even using like code snippets, but we handle like all the cross-linking, all of the image generation.
So, you know, I think there is a chance that like maybe it's too niche and maybe it's too technical, but these are just like some examples of other companies we've worked with in the similar realm.
Yeah, yeah, this would be a good one to see even a couple because I think
That's very similar to what we do in terms of buyer persona and companies, even if the product is completely different.
But yeah, I think that would be good.
And also, I think you mentioned, like I was looking at your pricing page before, but then just now you mentioned the content refresh.
That is also something that we are really interested in doing now, refreshing the content.

So maybe something that we could start working even like tomorrow is if you have like a proposal on taking all the content that you see on the website, as you see something's older at 2022, and what will it take for you to refresh it and republish it with a new date?
Because our initial idea was just like, let's republish it with a new date.
But yeah, I think doing a little bit of refresh to make sure that still...
We're not talking about things that don't exist anymore.
That would be golden.
And maybe it's like an easy pilot that we can run.
If that is successful, then we can do more fresh content.
But that is actually a need that we have like yesterday, content refresh.
And it's kind of funny that you actually noticed that.
Yeah, that makes a lot of sense.
It's we're typically like standardizing how we work with each client with the phase one, phase two approach.
But I think like especially Q1 next year, we'll start to find some more flexible ways of working.
And we're even launching like an AI visibility and monitoring product that we're going to use for every single client.
So I think like that might be a better timing if we wanted to go deviate from the phase one, phase two.
But I think, you know, one thing that would be.
I to go through is like an example of how we created a piece for augment code so that you can sort of see like what's behind the engine for a technical customer.
Does that sound helpful?
Yeah, definitely.
That was something that we can do for sure as well.
Perfect.
So, you know, augment code, good editorial example, lovable, also good example of a client where we've created editorial content and then even programmatic content.
So we actually interact with the lovable API to create these different templates using their product and optimizing them for SEO and GEO.
So what are people searching for the most?
Right.
And how can we have the best text on each of these examples?
So just like showing you how we can be flexible around different use cases with ramp, we did a programmatic use case for their vendor directory.
So we'll use their internal spend data and now you start to see ramp.
You know, show up in these tool comparison pages because of the way they want to have authority with business spending.
And so moving into our process here, you know, in phase one, this is just a good example to use since it's really simple, engines in the business travel space.
And so the first thing that our team is doing is we're ingesting resources from their team so that we can start to develop these artifacts in our platform that I'll show you.

But then we're also, you know, just doing the keyword analysis and connecting it to search volume and competitor content so that we can start to think through what are the topics where we want to concentrate creating content assignments that make the most sense for their business, right?
And where is there an opportunity for quick wins?
And so we're developing this content OS and creating a huge backlog of assignments for our team to recommend prioritization around, but your team to, you know, confirm.
Like what you want us to focus on first, and then we're just sharing drafts with your team to review and improve.

And so what happens internally for us to be able to do, you know, this work in our platform is we actually are creating these artifacts that go super deep and provide context for every piece of content that we create.
So with Engine, they're in the business travel space, and they wanted to verticalize a lot of the content to the teams that they sell to.

And we've got some insight on like why Engine, and then we go super deep into the jobs to be done, the fears and motivations, basically like everything this construction operations manager might be going in to chat GPT for that's connected to their products and services, right?

And so we have all this context around who they're writing for, we have all this context in who's writing, and then we also have context around the specifics of how the content, you know, should be written.
So we have like a proofreader checklist.
And then writing guidelines that are basically going to go as deep as like, here's how you should mention the competitors.
Here's the specific terminology that we like to use.
You get the idea.
And then it moves into the actual pipelines for content creation.
Any questions on this first part?
I'll move into the workflows next.
And Tyler, quickly, do you compete directly with Daydream?
You know, Daydream's come up a few times, and I got to do a little bit more research there.
I need to learn more about what they do to give you a good answer.
Got it.
Yeah, yeah.
So just to be upfront, so I did try to, before GrowthX, I did had a call with them quite early this year.
But basically, they said like, oh, we, this is too niche, and we won't be able to work with you.
But it's good that you already...
You have like Redis and some other tech-specific clients.
So hopefully, this is not an issue for you.
And then...
Yeah.
Go ahead.
Yeah.
I was just going to say, and, you know, maybe it is the same, right?
For us, I would want to check with the team and do some more analysis around like the search opportunity against your competition.
But that's just in the spirit of like making sure, you know, you choose a partner that's going to benefit you in all the ways you're looking for.
And sometimes like SEO, GEO, you know, if it's that alone, you know, maybe there's money spent in better places.
Got it.
Simone, you had something?
Yeah.
No, question was, and so you mentioned that you can directly post into our CMS or even if you don't, mean, you can just give us a talk and we can copy-taste it.
But do you also...
Push content to other places other than the company's CMS.
Because my fear is always like if I outsource content creation to a company, then of course, yeah, we're going to go to a review and everything, but then it's back to us and doing the review.
And we only have so many marketing folks and technical folks that can review and then it's kind of like a bottleneck.
And then it's always hard to come up with what to say, what not to say, especially if it is in our website.
That's why we are actually pretty slow in producing content because we always want to be the content to be perfect.

But then if the content is actually not on our website, maybe it's like some other dev.com, Reddit or something that it looks like it was created by other people, then we are not so strict because it's not our umbrella, but it's still going to help drive EO, GEO, because it's still going to talk about our company.
Do you also do that?
Or not?
Or have you had this type of concern from other companies?
Yeah, I would say like we don't really publish outside of your marketing site today.
May change in the future.
But sometimes what companies do is they don't have it in their main nav and they maybe set up a slash guides for us that's separate from their blog that might be more engineering focused.

So that it still can be a part of your sitemap, can still get indexed, but a little bit harder, you know, can still get cited in AI answers, but a little bit harder for someone to find who like went straight to the vCluster website.

And the reason they do it is the same reason because the content is more like SEO, AEO type of a contest of people coming to the website might find that like, oh, it's just doing that for SEO, AEO, or what is the reason they're doing like a separate path that is not.
Yeah, it's exactly your concern.
We want to be perceived as like a highly technical organization.
We want to have content that's basically written by developers.
And a good example with Augment Code here would be, you know, they're trying to be seen for companies that have large code bases who want to use an AI tool for an enterprise use case, right?
That has like a large context window, a lot of understanding.
And so, you know, Augment Code, we have like seven citations in this answer.
And it's different, right, for you, for me, for everybody each day.
But we just re-go really deep and thorough so that we can connect, you know, your content to all the different variations of how someone would prompt ChatGPT and have a conversation with it.
And not just ChatGPT, right, other LLMs.
Sure.
But I know we're almost at time.
Do you have a few minutes to see the workflows or do you have a hard stop?
No, a few minutes.
We can quickly see it.
And then I think, before I forget, I think the two big asks for me would be content refresh and then competitive.
Like, you sure that you can do ClickUp versus this?
We are also looking at creating content like that, big clusters versus so-and-so.
So that could be something that we can start collaborating quickly and then get some quick wins before we go full.
I mean, to create content from scratch, then you will need, you know, the personas, the style guys, you know, that.
And then might now it's also like vacation and everything.
So it might take a bit longer.
Makes sense.
So what happens in terms of creating content, we use, you know, those artifacts that I went through.

We can actually ingest resources into our knowledge base or store our research here and send a knowledge graph so that we can semantically bring it in to any new piece of content that we create, make the engine smarter over time.

And so if you think about us creating a brief for an assignment, we're taking those artifacts and then maybe a specific topic or direction or keyword, and then we're generating this really robust brief for co-pilot versus cursor with the proposed content structure.
But in the background, we're actually looking at organic results, keyword traffic, search intent, ranking factors, competitor content, and all of that informs the output.
So we've got all these different workflows.
And what's cool is this is using the SEMrush API, the SERP API.
So using a bunch of different prompts within this one workflow, and then it's also, you know, changing the path based on the scenario.
Did I feed it a URL?
Did I feed it a keyword?
And so this is part of like our secret sauce is we've just developed these really robust workflows that in a lot of instances are agentic.
And then we have our experts in the loop who at any step of this process, right, can make changes and then process to the next step.
And so, you know, in this example, we're leveraging that brief and then doing deep research for 24 minutes.
We're drafting the article template and it's going through multiple iterations as it runs these evals that are connected to accuracy.
And the writing guidelines that we've uploaded and things like the direction of the content, you know, is it being written?

And for the right audience, well, and then by breaking it into these distinct steps, you know, that's how can we can be really certain around accuracy around how it accommodates to the guidelines that we've set up, because we're running these things multiple times, right?
After the article's drafted, it's then validating all that content against the writing guidelines.
It's then fact checking every single passage.
So this is an interesting workflow where, you know, we'll split the article into chunks.
And then for each passage, we'll do additional research that's focused on making sure, okay, we can actually validate this so that there's no penalty around it being inaccurate.
And even if there's some nuance, you know, we'll actually suggest a rewrite that the editor can then accept and adjust.
Very quick.
I apologize.
It's a lot to show.
But this is the way that, like, the workflows are working in tandem with the...
Editors and the content experts.
Perfect.
Yeah, think Rahul has to drop.
But yeah, no, that was a great overview.
And yeah, I think if you could, yeah, see if you can do those things, like, you competitive content refresh, maybe next year, that would be great.
And in the meantime, we'll see, like, yeah, if we can do something with content and, you know, work with our web team and, you know, have a special place, know, big guys and things like that for SEO.
Yeah, it makes a lot of sense.
Yeah, and then, yeah, if you can send some example, maybe from Redis or that you have produced, that would be great.
So we can see the level of...
of details.
Maybe this would just work.
Yeah, absolutely.
Great to make the intro today.
I'll send you a bunch of great resources and we'll send you stuff that isn't relevant.

And then we can just follow up to either dive deeper into the opportunity or talk a little more tactically about what the partnership would look like so that you can think about, you know, next year and how you might want to proceed with us or go a different direction in Q1.
Awesome.
All right.
Thank you, Tyler.
Nice to meet you.
Thank you.
Looking forward to working with you.
Yeah, have a great weekend and talk soon.
See ya.
Okay, bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Evaluate GrowthX for Loft's SEO and content needs.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=104.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Loft's SEO is flattening** due to AI search; they need to scale content from \~5 articles/month to drive growth.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=177.0)
  - [**GrowthX offers a full-service model** (strategy, 5 articles/week, CMS publishing) using a proprietary AI platform and in-house writers.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=343.0)
  - [**A pilot project was proposed** for Q1 2026, focusing on content refreshes and competitive "vs." articles to achieve quick wins.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1571.0)
  - [**GrowthX must validate the market opportunity** for Loft's niche product, VCluster, to ensure a partnership is viable.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1282.0)

## Topics

### Loft's Content & SEO Challenge

  - [**Problem:** Organic growth has flattened since AI search became prevalent.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=177.0)
  - [**Product:** VCluster, a virtualization layer for Kubernetes.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=702.0)
      - [**Analogy:** VMware for physical infra → VCluster for Kubernetes.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=702.0)
      - [**Benefit:** Reduces operational complexity and cost vs. managing many separate K8s clusters.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=732.0)
  - [**Target Audience:** Technical roles (Platform/Cloud Architects, DevOps, SRE) in digital-native companies.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=776.0)
  - [**Past Experience:** A previous agency (Daydream) declined to work with Loft, deeming the market too niche.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1282.0)

### GrowthX's Solution & Process

  - [**Service Model:** An end-to-end partner handling strategy, execution, and monitoring.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=343.0)
  - [**Volume:** Aims for 5 articles/week (new or refresh).](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)
  - [**Execution:** Manages staging and publishing directly in Loft's CMS.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)
  - [**Team:** In-house delivery team (\~2/3 of 90 employees).](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=622.0)
  - [**Technical Clients:** Redis, Abnormal Security, Airbyte, Sentinel-1, Surge AI.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=656.0)
  - [**Internal Process:**](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1147.0)
      - [**Context Engineering:** Ingests client resources to build a "Content OS" with deep artifacts (personas, writing guidelines, competitor strategy).](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1206.0)
      - [**AI-Powered Workflows:** Uses agentic AI for research, drafting, and fact-checking, with human experts in-the-loop for oversight.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1671.0)

### Proposed Pilot Project

  - [**Goal:** Achieve quick wins to validate the partnership before a full engagement.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1571.0)
  - [**Scope:**](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1571.0)
      - [**Content Refresh:** Update older articles (e.g., from 2022) with new information and publish dates.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=972.0)
      - [**Competitive Content:** Create "VCluster vs. \[Competitor\]" articles.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1571.0)
  - [**Publishing Strategy:**](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1367.0)
      - [**Loft's Concern:** High internal review burden for content on the main site.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1384.0)
      - [**GrowthX's Suggestion:** Use a separate sub-path (e.g., `/guides`) for SEO content to maintain brand perception while still benefiting from indexing.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1446.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Tyler (GrowthX):**](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1863.0)
      - [Send technical content examples (Redis, Airbyte).](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1851.0)
      - [Analyze the market opportunity for VCluster to confirm viability.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1282.0)
      - [Propose a pilot project plan (content refresh, competitive articles) for Q1 2026.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1571.0)
  - [**Simone (Loft):**](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1571.0)
      - [Discuss the pilot project internally with Rahul.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1571.0)
      - [Evaluate the separate `/guides` publishing strategy with the web team.](https://fathom.video/share/9nqpwhTU8yAjHxNkiByQhGGL-aaYBUJx?tab=summary&timestamp=1826.0)
